lessons.subject: Technology
lessons.title: Grade 7 The design process — Core teaching
learning_objectives: Apply the design/problem-solving process to the design process.
Build, test, debug or evaluate a solution using appropriate tools and terminology.
Communicate decisions, results and improvements clearly.
lessons.lesson_body:
In this lesson learners study the design process using clear classroom examples. CAPS focus: Apply the design/problem-solving process to the design process. Key vocabulary: design, process, TECHNOLOGY, GRADES. Follow the design process, name materials and tools, and explain how the solution meets the brief. Grounding snippet: --- page 7 ---
TECHNOLOGY GRADES 7-9
2
CURRICULUM AND ASSESSMENT POLICY STATEMENT (CAPS)
4.4 Programme of assessment ....................................................................................................... Before finishing, learners restate the method, complete the practice set and compare answers with a partner.
lessons.explanation:

CURRICULUM AND ASSESSMENT POLICY STATEMENT (CAPS)
4.4 Programme of assessment ....................................................................................................... Before finishing, learners restate the method, complete the practice set and compare answers with a partner.

worked_examples.question: During a class experiment on the design process, the thermometer reading rises from 22°C to 29°C. What changed?
step_by_step_solution: Compare the starting and ending measurements.
State the quantity that increased.
Link the change to the process being studied.
Write the answer using the unit from the apparatus.
worked_examples.answer: The temperature increased by 7°C during the process.

worked_examples.question: A learner says the design process happens without energy. What correction should the teacher give?
step_by_step_solution: Identify the incorrect claim.
Name the form of energy or transfer involved.
Give one classroom example.
State the corrected explanation clearly.
worked_examples.answer: The Design Process requires energy transfer; without it the process cannot occur.

practice_questions.question_text: Which statement about the design process is supported by observation?

options.A: Results can be guessed without apparatus.
options.B: The measurement changed when the conditions changed.
options.C: Evidence is optional in an investigation.
options.D: A hypothesis cannot be tested.

practice_questions.question_text: Which apparatus is most appropriate for a lesson on the design process?

options.A: A ruler used to measure temperature
options.B: A compass used to measure mass
options.C: The tool named in the lesson method for measuring the quantity
options.D: Any object chosen at random

practice_questions.explanation: Each quantity needs a suitable measuring tool.

practice_questions.question_text: Why should learners record results when studying the design process?

options.A: So they can ignore unexpected results
options.B: So they can change the data to match a guess
options.C: So they can skip the conclusion
options.D: So patterns can be compared and conclusions checked

answer_key.correct_answer_text: The measurement changed when the conditions changed.
The tool named in the lesson method for measuring the quantity
So patterns can be compared and conclusions checked

remediation_hints.hint_text: When topic vocabulary confusion appears, return to the worked example for the design process and solve one smaller part before attempting the full question.
remediation_hints.example: Label the diagram and point to the evidence that supports the answer.

extension_prompts: Create a new Grade 7 example about the design process and teach it to a partner using the standard approach.
Write two exam-style questions for 7.TECH.1.1 and explain why your marking memo is correct.
Connect the design process to a real situation in your community and justify your answer with evidence.
lessons.teacher_notes: Pacing: 15 minutes teaching, 10 minutes guided practice, 10 minutes independent practice for the design process. Use the standard variant materials first, then move to the practice set. Common errors to watch: topic vocabulary confusion, procedure without conceptual understanding. Assessment cue: ask learners to explain one method aloud before marking the exit ticket. Subject focus: technology.
lessons.parent_notes: Ask your child to teach you one example about the design process without looking at the notes. Help them restate the main vocabulary and check whether their answer matches the question. If they are stuck, use a household object to model the idea rather than giving the final answer immediately. This supports technology learning at home.

worked_examples.question: During a class experiment on the design process, the thermometer reading rises from 22°C to 29°C. What changed?
step_by_step_solution: Compare the starting and ending measurements.
State the quantity that increased.
Link the change to the process being studied.
Write the answer using the unit from the apparatus.
worked_examples.answer: The temperature increased by 7°C during the process.

worked_examples.question: A learner says the design process happens without energy. What correction should the teacher give?
step_by_step_solution: Identify the incorrect claim.
Name the form of energy or transfer involved.
Give one classroom example.
State the corrected explanation clearly.
worked_examples.answer: The Design Process requires energy transfer; without it the process cannot occur.

practice_questions.question_text: Which statement about the design process is supported by observation?

options.A: The measurement changed when the conditions changed.
options.B: Results can be guessed without apparatus.
options.C: Evidence is optional in an investigation.
options.D: A hypothesis cannot be tested.

practice_questions.question_text: Which apparatus is most appropriate for a lesson on the design process?

options.A: A ruler used to measure temperature
options.B: The tool named in the lesson method for measuring the quantity
options.C: A compass used to measure mass
options.D: Any object chosen at random

practice_questions.explanation: Each quantity needs a suitable measuring tool.

practice_questions.question_text: Why should learners record results when studying the design process?

options.A: So they can ignore unexpected results
options.B: So they can change the data to match a guess
options.C: So patterns can be compared and conclusions checked
options.D: So they can skip the conclusion

answer_key.correct_answer_text: The measurement changed when the conditions changed.
The tool named in the lesson method for measuring the quantity
So patterns can be compared and conclusions checked

remediation_hints.hint_text: When procedure without conceptual understanding appears, return to the worked example for the design process and solve one smaller part before attempting the full question.
remediation_hints.example: Label the diagram and point to the evidence that supports the answer.

extension_prompts: Create a new Grade 7 example about the design process and teach it to a partner using the visual approach.
Write two exam-style questions for 7.TECH.1.1 and explain why your marking memo is correct.
Connect the design process to a real situation in your community and justify your answer with evidence.
lessons.teacher_notes: Pacing: 15 minutes teaching, 10 minutes guided practice, 10 minutes independent practice for the design process. Use the visual variant materials first, then move to the practice set. Common errors to watch: topic vocabulary confusion, procedure without conceptual understanding. Assessment cue: ask learners to explain one method aloud before marking the exit ticket. Subject focus: technology.
lessons.parent_notes: Ask your child to teach you one example about the design process without looking at the notes. Help them restate the main vocabulary and check whether their answer matches the question. If they are stuck, use a household object to model the idea rather than giving the final answer immediately. This supports technology learning at home.

worked_examples.answer: Graphic Communication requires energy transfer; without it the process cannot occur.

options.A: Results can be guessed without apparatus.

worked_examples.question: During a class experiment on processing, the thermometer reading rises from 22°C to 29°C. What changed?
step_by_step_solution: Compare the starting and ending measurements.
State the quantity that increased.
Link the change to the process being studied.
Write the answer using the unit from the apparatus.
worked_examples.answer: The temperature increased by 7°C during the process.

worked_examples.question: A learner says processing happens without energy. What correction should the teacher give?
step_by_step_solution: Identify the incorrect claim.
Name the form of energy or transfer involved.
Give one classroom example.
State the corrected explanation clearly.
worked_examples.answer: Processing requires energy transfer; without it the process cannot occur.

worked_examples.question: During a class experiment on materials and tools, the thermometer reading rises from 22°C to 29°C. What changed?
step_by_step_solution: Compare the starting and ending measurements.
State the quantity that increased.
Link the change to the process being studied.
Write the answer using the unit from the apparatus.
worked_examples.answer: The temperature increased by 7°C during the process.

worked_examples.question: A learner says materials and tools happens without energy. What correction should the teacher give?
step_by_step_solution: Identify the incorrect claim.
Name the form of energy or transfer involved.
Give one classroom example.
State the corrected explanation clearly.
worked_examples.answer: Materials And Tools requires energy transfer; without it the process cannot occur.

practice_questions.question_text: Which statement about materials and tools is supported by observation?

options.A: Results can be guessed without apparatus.
options.B: The measurement changed when the conditions changed.
options.C: Evidence is optional in an investigation.
options.D: A hypothesis cannot be tested.

practice_questions.question_text: Which apparatus is most appropriate for a lesson on materials and tools?

options.A: A ruler used to measure temperature
options.B: A compass used to measure mass
options.C: The tool named in the lesson method for measuring the quantity
options.D: Any object chosen at random

practice_questions.explanation: Each quantity needs a suitable measuring tool.

practice_questions.question_text: Why should learners record results when studying materials and tools?

options.A: So they can ignore unexpected results
options.B: So they can change the data to match a guess
options.C: So they can skip the conclusion
options.D: So patterns can be compared and conclusions checked

answer_key.correct_answer_text: The measurement changed when the conditions changed.
The tool named in the lesson method for measuring the quantity
So patterns can be compared and conclusions checked

remediation_hints.hint_text: When topic vocabulary confusion appears, return to the worked example for materials and tools and solve one smaller part before attempting the full question.
remediation_hints.example: Label the diagram and point to the evidence that supports the answer.

extension_prompts: Create a new Grade 7 example about materials and tools and teach it to a partner using the standard approach.
Write two exam-style questions for 7.TECH.2.2 and explain why your marking memo is correct.
Connect materials and tools to a real situation in your community and justify your answer with evidence.
lessons.teacher_notes: Pacing: 15 minutes teaching, 10 minutes guided practice, 10 minutes independent practice for materials and tools. Use the standard variant materials first, then move to the practice set. Common errors to watch: topic vocabulary confusion, procedure without conceptual understanding. Assessment cue: ask learners to explain one method aloud before marking the exit ticket. Subject focus: technology.
lessons.parent_notes: Ask your child to teach you one example about materials and tools without looking at the notes. Help them restate the main vocabulary and check whether their answer matches the question. If they are stuck, use a household object to model the idea rather than giving the final answer immediately. This supports technology learning at home.

worked_examples.question: During a class experiment on systems and control, the thermometer reading rises from 22°C to 29°C. What changed?
step_by_step_solution: Compare the starting and ending measurements.
State the quantity that increased.
Link the change to the process being studied.
Write the answer using the unit from the apparatus.
worked_examples.answer: The temperature increased by 7°C during the process.

worked_examples.question: A learner says systems and control happens without energy. What correction should the teacher give?
step_by_step_solution: Identify the incorrect claim.
Name the form of energy or transfer involved.
Give one classroom example.
State the corrected explanation clearly.
worked_examples.answer: Systems And Control requires energy transfer; without it the process cannot occur.

practice_questions.question_text: Which statement about systems and control is supported by observation?

options.A: Results can be guessed without apparatus.
options.B: Evidence is optional in an investigation.
options.C: The measurement changed when the conditions changed.
options.D: A hypothesis cannot be tested.

practice_questions.question_text: Which apparatus is most appropriate for a lesson on systems and control?

options.A: A ruler used to measure temperature
options.B: A compass used to measure mass
options.C: Any object chosen at random
options.D: The tool named in the lesson method for measuring the quantity

practice_questions.explanation: Each quantity needs a suitable measuring tool.

practice_questions.question_text: Why should learners record results when studying systems and control?

options.A: So patterns can be compared and conclusions checked
options.B: So they can ignore unexpected results
options.C: So they can change the data to match a guess
options.D: So they can skip the conclusion

answer_key.correct_answer_text: The measurement changed when the conditions changed.
The tool named in the lesson method for measuring the quantity
So patterns can be compared and conclusions checked

remediation_hints.hint_text: When procedure without conceptual understanding appears, return to the worked example for systems and control and solve one smaller part before attempting the full question.
remediation_hints.example: Label the diagram and point to the evidence that supports the answer.

extension_prompts: Create a new Grade 7 example about systems and control and teach it to a partner using the visual approach.
Write two exam-style questions for 7.TECH.3.1 and explain why your marking memo is correct.
Connect systems and control to a real situation in your community and justify your answer with evidence.

Use the visual variant materials first, then move to the practice set. Common errors to watch: topic vocabulary confusion, procedure without conceptual understanding. Assessment cue: ask learners to explain one method aloud before marking the exit ticket. Subject focus: technology.

Key vocabulary: Systems, control, TECHNOLOGY, GRADES. Follow the design process, name materials and tools, and explain how the solution meets the brief. Grounding snippet: --- page 15 ---
TECHNOLOGY GRADES 7-9
10
CURRICULUM AND ASSESSMENT POLICY STATEMENT (CAPS)
• Through practical projects using a variety of technological skills (investigating, designing, making, evaluating 
and communica Before finishing, learners restate the method, complete the practice set and compare answers with a partner.

worked_examples.question: During a class experiment on systems and control, the thermometer reading rises from 22°C to 29°C. What changed?
step_by_step_solution: Compare the starting and ending measurements.
State the quantity that increased.
Link the change to the process being studied.
Write the answer using the unit from the apparatus.
worked_examples.answer: The temperature increased by 7°C during the process.

worked_examples.question: A learner says systems and control happens without energy. What correction should the teacher give?
step_by_step_solution: Identify the incorrect claim.
Name the form of energy or transfer involved.
Give one classroom example.
State the corrected explanation clearly.
worked_examples.answer: Systems And Control requires energy transfer; without it the process cannot occur.

practice_questions.question_text: Which statement about systems and control is supported by observation?

options.A: The measurement changed when the conditions changed.
options.B: Results can be guessed without apparatus.
options.C: Evidence is optional in an investigation.
options.D: A hypothesis cannot be tested.

practice_questions.question_text: Which apparatus is most appropriate for a lesson on systems and control?

options.A: A ruler used to measure temperature
options.B: The tool named in the lesson method for measuring the quantity
options.C: A compass used to measure mass
options.D: Any object chosen at random

practice_questions.explanation: Each quantity needs a suitable measuring tool.

practice_questions.question_text: Why should learners record results when studying systems and control?

options.A: So they can ignore unexpected results
options.B: So they can change the data to match a guess
options.C: So patterns can be compared and conclusions checked
options.D: So they can skip the conclusion

answer_key.correct_answer_text: The measurement changed when the conditions changed.
The tool named in the lesson method for measuring the quantity
So patterns can be compared and conclusions checked

remediation_hints.hint_text: When procedure without conceptual understanding appears, return to the worked example for systems and control and solve one smaller part before attempting the full question.
remediation_hints.example: Label the diagram and point to the evidence that supports the answer.

extension_prompts: Create a new Grade 7 example about systems and control and teach it to a partner using the visual approach.
Write two exam-style questions for 7.TECH.3.1 and explain why your marking memo is correct.
Connect systems and control to a real situation in your community and justify your answer with evidence.
lessons.teacher_notes: Pacing: 15 minutes teaching, 10 minutes guided practice, 10 minutes independent practice for systems and control. Use the visual variant materials first, then move to the practice set. Common errors to watch: topic vocabulary confusion, procedure without conceptual understanding. Assessment cue: ask learners to explain one method aloud before marking the exit ticket. Subject focus: technology.
lessons.parent_notes: Ask your child to teach you one example about systems and control without looking at the notes. Help them restate the main vocabulary and check whether their answer matches the question. If they are stuck, use a household object to model the idea rather than giving the final answer immediately. This supports technology learning at home.

worked_examples.question: During a class experiment on electrical systems, the thermometer reading rises from 22°C to 29°C. What changed?
step_by_step_solution: Compare the starting and ending measurements.
State the quantity that increased.
Link the change to the process being studied.
Write the answer using the unit from the apparatus.
worked_examples.answer: The temperature increased by 7°C during the process.

worked_examples.question: A learner says electrical systems happens without energy. What correction should the teacher give?
step_by_step_solution: Identify the incorrect claim.
Name the form of energy or transfer involved.
Give one classroom example.
State the corrected explanation clearly.
worked_examples.answer: Electrical Systems requires energy transfer; without it the process cannot occur.

practice_questions.question_text: Which statement about electrical systems is supported by observation?

options.A: Results can be guessed without apparatus.
options.B: The measurement changed when the conditions changed.
options.C: Evidence is optional in an investigation.
options.D: A hypothesis cannot be tested.

practice_questions.question_text: Which apparatus is most appropriate for a lesson on electrical systems?

options.A: A ruler used to measure temperature
options.B: A compass used to measure mass
options.C: The tool named in the lesson method for measuring the quantity
options.D: Any object chosen at random

practice_questions.explanation: Each quantity needs a suitable measuring tool.

practice_questions.question_text: Why should learners record results when studying electrical systems?

options.A: So they can ignore unexpected results
options.B: So they can change the data to match a guess
options.C: So they can skip the conclusion
options.D: So patterns can be compared and conclusions checked

answer_key.correct_answer_text: The measurement changed when the conditions changed.
The tool named in the lesson method for measuring the quantity
So patterns can be compared and conclusions checked

remediation_hints.hint_text: When topic vocabulary confusion appears, return to the worked example for electrical systems and solve one smaller part before attempting the full question.
remediation_hints.example: Label the diagram and point to the evidence that supports the answer.

extension_prompts: Create a new Grade 7 example about electrical systems and teach it to a partner using the real world sa approach.

Connect electrical systems to a real situation in your community and justify your answer with evidence.

lessons.lesson_body:
A short South African classroom story introduces assessment before learners practise the skill. CAPS focus: Apply the design/problem-solving process to assessment. Key vocabulary: Assessment, MTMTMTMTMTMTMTMTMTMTMTMTMTMTMT, MTMTMTMTMTMTMTMTMTMTMT, MTMTMTMTMTMTMTMTMTMTMTMTMTMTMTMTMTMTMTMTMTMTMTMT. Follow the design process, name materials and tools, and explain how the solution meets the brief. Grounding snippet: --- page 1 ---
/MT66/MT97/MT115/MT105/MT99/MT32/MT69/MT100/MT117/MT99/MT97/MT116/MT105/MT111/MT110
/MT68/MT101/MT112/MT97/MT114/MT116/MT109/MT101/MT110/MT116/MT58
/MT82/MT69/MT80/MT85/MT66/MT76/MT73/MT67/MT32/MT79/MT70/M Before finishing, learners restate the method, complete the practice set and compare answers with a partner.

worked_examples.question: During a class experiment on assessment, the thermometer reading rises from 22°C to 29°C. What changed?
step_by_step_solution: Compare the starting and ending measurements.
State the quantity that increased.
Link the change to the process being studied.
Write the answer using the unit from the apparatus.
worked_examples.answer: The temperature increased by 7°C during the process.

worked_examples.question: A learner says assessment happens without energy. What correction should the teacher give?
step_by_step_solution: Identify the incorrect claim.
Name the form of energy or transfer involved.
Give one classroom example.
State the corrected explanation clearly.
worked_examples.answer: Assessment requires energy transfer; without it the process cannot occur.

practice_questions.question_text: Which statement about assessment is supported by observation?

options.A: Results can be guessed without apparatus.
options.B: Evidence is optional in an investigation.
options.C: A hypothesis cannot be tested.
options.D: The measurement changed when the conditions changed.

practice_questions.question_text: Which apparatus is most appropriate for a lesson on assessment?

options.A: The tool named in the lesson method for measuring the quantity
options.B: A ruler used to measure temperature
options.C: A compass used to measure mass
options.D: Any object chosen at random

practice_questions.explanation: Each quantity needs a suitable measuring tool.

practice_questions.question_text: Why should learners record results when studying assessment?

options.A: So they can ignore unexpected results
options.B: So patterns can be compared and conclusions checked
options.C: So they can change the data to match a guess
options.D: So they can skip the conclusion

answer_key.correct_answer_text: The measurement changed when the conditions changed.
The tool named in the lesson method for measuring the quantity
So patterns can be compared and conclusions checked

remediation_hints.hint_text: When topic vocabulary confusion appears, return to the worked example for assessment and solve one smaller part before attempting the full question.
remediation_hints.example: Label the diagram and point to the evidence that supports the answer.

extension_prompts: Create a new Grade 7 example about assessment and teach it to a partner using the story approach.
Write two exam-style questions for 7.TECH.3.4 and explain why your marking memo is correct.
Connect assessment to a real situation in your community and justify your answer with evidence.
lessons.teacher_notes: Pacing: 15 minutes teaching, 10 minutes guided practice, 10 minutes independent practice for assessment. Use the story variant materials first, then move to the practice set. Common errors to watch: topic vocabulary confusion, procedure without conceptual understanding. Assessment cue: ask learners to explain one method aloud before marking the exit ticket. Subject focus: technology.
lessons.parent_notes: Ask your child to teach you one example about assessment without looking at the notes. Help them restate the main vocabulary and check whether their answer matches the question. If they are stuck, use a household object to model the idea rather than giving the final answer immediately. This supports technology learning at home.

worked_examples.question: During a class experiment on technology project, the thermometer reading rises from 22°C to 29°C. What changed?
step_by_step_solution: Compare the starting and ending measurements.
State the quantity that increased.
Link the change to the process being studied.
Write the answer using the unit from the apparatus.
worked_examples.answer: The temperature increased by 7°C during the process.

worked_examples.question: A learner says technology project happens without energy. What correction should the teacher give?
step_by_step_solution: Identify the incorrect claim.
Name the form of energy or transfer involved.
Give one classroom example.
State the corrected explanation clearly.
worked_examples.answer: Technology Project requires energy transfer; without it the process cannot occur.

practice_questions.question_text: Which statement about technology project is supported by observation?

options.A: Results can be guessed without apparatus.
options.B: The measurement changed when the conditions changed.

options.D: A hypothesis cannot be tested.

options.A: A ruler used to measure temperature

practice_questions.explanation: Each quantity needs a suitable measuring tool.

options.A: So they can ignore unexpected results

answer_key.correct_answer_text: The measurement changed when the conditions changed.
The tool named in the lesson method for measuring the quantity
So patterns can be compared and conclusions checked

worked_examples.question: During a class experiment on evaluation and communication, the thermometer reading rises from 22°C to 29°C. What changed?

State the quantity that increased.

worked_examples.answer: The temperature increased by 7°C during the process.

worked_examples.question: A learner says evaluation and communication happens without energy. What correction should the teacher give?
step_by_step_solution: Identify the incorrect claim.
Name the form of energy or transfer involved.
Give one classroom example.
State the corrected explanation clearly.
worked_examples.answer: Evaluation And Communication requires energy transfer; without it the process cannot occur.

practice_questions.question_text: Which statement about evaluation and communication is supported by observation?

options.A: The measurement changed when the conditions changed.
options.B: Results can be guessed without apparatus.

options.A: A ruler used to measure temperature

practice_questions.explanation: Each quantity needs a suitable measuring tool.

answer_key.correct_answer_text: The measurement changed when the conditions changed.
The tool named in the lesson method for measuring the quantity
So patterns can be compared and conclusions checked
